_purpose: Local economy timeline for the AL Black Belt regional cluster (Dallas, Marengo, Sumter, Greene, Perry, Hale, Wilcox, Lowndes counties). Covers 1817-1960 across six economic eras — from Creek and Choctaw cession through the plantation economy, Civil War destruction, sharecropping, boll weevil devastation, and the Great Migration depopulation. The Black Belt's economic story is the American South's story in its most extreme form: wealth built on enslaved labor, total collapse after emancipation, and a century of poverty that drove the majority of the population north.
display_name: AL Black Belt — Local Economy Timeline

economic_eras.era_name: The Cotton Rush: Creek and Choctaw Cession to King Cotton
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Plantation cotton on newly ceded Native lands; rapid importation of enslaved laborers from Upper South states; Selma and Demopolis as river shipping centers; land speculation
economic_eras.description: The Black Belt economy was born in dispossession. The Creek War of 1813-14 and the Treaty of Fort Jackson opened the eastern counties (Dallas, Perry, Lowndes, Wilcox) to American settlement. The Choctaw removal under the Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830) opened the western counties (Marengo, Sumter, Greene). Planters from Virginia, the Carolinas, and Georgia rushed in, bringing enslaved laborers — sometimes hundreds at a time — to convert the dark prairie to cotton. The domestic slave trade was the engine: slave traders moved thousands of enslaved people from the Upper South to the Alabama Black Belt during this period. Land speculation was rampant. A planter who arrived in 1820 with capital and enslaved labor could be wealthy by 1830. The Alabama River and Tombigbee River provided shipping routes to Mobile, connecting the Black Belt to the global cotton market.

county_specifics.note: Formed 1818. Selma (founded 1820) became the commercial center on the Alabama River bluff. Rapid plantation development.
Formed 1818. Vine and Olive Colony (1817) at Demopolis — French Napoleonic exiles who abandoned vineyards for cotton. Tombigbee River shipping.
Formed 1832 from Choctaw cession. Among the last Black Belt counties opened. Immediate plantation development.
Formed 1819. Rapid conversion of prairie to cotton. One of the wealthiest counties by 1840.
Formed 1819. Marion became an educational center (Judson College, Howard College). Mixed prairie-hills economy.
Part of Greene County until 1867. Southern half was plantation country; northern hills were yeoman farms.
Formed 1819. Pettway plantation at Gee's Bend. Alabama River corridor plantations.
Formed 1830. Developed the most extreme enslaved-to-free ratio in the cluster — over 80% enslaved by 1860.

key_infrastructure.name: Alabama River steamboat service
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected Selma and the eastern counties to Mobile. Cotton was shipped downstream; supplies came upstream. Selma's wharf became the commercial hub.

key_infrastructure.name: Tombigbee River steamboat service
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected Demopolis and the western counties to Mobile via the Tombigbee-Alabama River system.

key_infrastructure.name: Cotton gins
key_infrastructure.impact: Gin houses at every plantation and crossroads. The gin made the cotton economy possible on prairie soil.

major_employers.name: Cotton plantations (forced labor)
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of enslaved people. The entire economy was built on forced labor. There was no 'free labor market' in the antebellum Black Belt.

wage_benchmarks.source: Tadman, Michael. Speculators and Slaves: Masters, Traders, and Slaves in the Old South. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1989.
Scarborough, William Kauffman. The Overseer: Plantation Management in the Old South. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1966.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: The Black Belt in the {decade}s was not settled — it was seized. {ancestor_name} came to land taken from the Creek or Choctaw within living memory, dark prairie soil that would grow cotton like nowhere else, and the labor to work it was bought and sold like the land itself.
economic_eras.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994). Tadman, Speculators and Slaves (1989).

economic_eras.era_name: The Plantation Peak: King Cotton and the Enslaved Majority
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Cotton monoculture at maximum intensity; enslaved populations exceeding 70-80% in most counties; planter aristocracy at its apogee; Selma as a commercial center; educational institutions at Marion
economic_eras.description: By 1860, the Alabama Black Belt was the epicenter of American cotton production and the most extreme example of the plantation slavery system. The wealth was staggering and entirely dependent on forced labor. Dallas County alone had over 25,000 enslaved people. Greene, Marengo, Sumter, and Lowndes counties had enslaved majorities exceeding 75-80%. The planters — a small class numbering perhaps 500-1,000 families across the cluster — controlled virtually all productive land, all political office, and all social institutions. The domestic slave trade brought a continuous flow of enslaved people from Virginia, Maryland, Kentucky, and the Carolinas. Selma was a major slave-trading center. The Black Belt's wealth funded Marion's colleges, Selma's banks, and the political influence that would drag Alabama into secession.

county_specifics.note: 25,000+ enslaved people. Selma as commercial and slave-trading center. Among the wealthiest counties in the South.
Enslaved population exceeded white by 3:1. Some of the largest plantations in Alabama. Gaineswood mansion.
Enslaved exceeded white 4:1. Extreme plantation concentration.
Among the highest enslaved population densities in the state.
Dual economy: plantations on the prairie, smaller farms in the hills. Marion's colleges funded by planter wealth.
Pettway plantation at Gee's Bend at peak operation. Alabama River corridor plantations at maximum extent.
Over 80% enslaved — functionally a forced-labor camp with a courthouse. Among the most extreme ratios in the South.

key_infrastructure.name: Alabama & Tennessee Rivers Railroad (planned)
key_infrastructure.impact: Railroad connections were being planned and partially built. Selma was emerging as a railroad junction, which would later make it the Confederacy's industrial center.

key_infrastructure.name: Selma commercial district
key_infrastructure.impact: Banks, cotton factors, slave traders, and merchant houses along Broad Street made Selma the commercial capital of the Black Belt.

major_employers.name: Cotton plantations (forced labor)
major_employers.employment_scale: The enslaved population of the eight counties collectively exceeded 100,000 people. The labor system was total — there was no free labor economy of any significance.

major_employers.name: Educational institutions (Marion)
major_employers.employment_scale: Small but culturally significant. Faculty, staff, and students supported a town economy.

wage_benchmarks.source: Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South. New York: Basic Books, 1986.

wage_benchmarks.occupation: Price of an enslaved prime field hand (male, 18-30)

economic_eras.narrative_hook: In {year}, the Black Belt was the richest agricultural region in America and the most unfree — {ancestor_name} lived in a county where the wealth was measured in cotton bales and human beings, and the two were produced by the same system.
economic_eras.source: Wright, Old South, New South (1986). Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994).

economic_eras.era_name: Civil War and Total Economic Collapse
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Confederate war production at Selma; agricultural disruption; conscription draining white male labor; enslaved labor system under pressure from self-liberation and Union advances; Wilson's Raid destroying Selma's industrial base
economic_eras.description: The Civil War destroyed the Black Belt's economy in stages. First, the blockade cut cotton exports to European markets. Second, conscription removed white overseers and small farmers. Third, enslaved people began self-liberating as Union forces approached — fleeing to Union lines, refusing labor, and disrupting the plantation system from within. Fourth, Selma's transformation into the Confederacy's second-most-important industrial center (after Richmond) made it a military target. The Selma Naval Foundry, Selma Arsenal, and Confederate Niter Works employed thousands of enslaved and impressed workers producing cannon, ammunition, and ironclad armor. On April 2, 1865, Union General James H. Wilson's cavalry captured Selma and burned the industrial complex. The destruction was total. The antebellum economic system — cotton, slavery, river commerce, planter wealth — was eliminated in four years.

county_specifics.note: Selma's Confederate arsenal destroyed by Wilson's Raid, April 1865. Massive property destruction.
Agricultural disruption. Cotton could not be shipped. Enslaved laborers began leaving plantations.
Economic disruption from conscription and trade blockade.
Agricultural collapse. Men gone to war; enslaved workers increasingly resistant.
Marion's colleges disrupted. Agricultural economy collapsed.
Gee's Bend plantation disrupted. River commerce halted.
The most enslaved county experienced the most dramatic labor disruption.

key_infrastructure.name: Selma Naval Foundry and Arsenal (Confederate)
key_infrastructure.impact: Produced cannon, shells, ironclad plate. Employed thousands of enslaved and impressed workers. Destroyed by Wilson's Raid, April 2, 1865.

major_employers.name: Confederate military service
major_employers.employment_scale: Most white men of military age served. Plantations run by overseers, women, and elderly men.

major_employers.name: Selma Confederate works (forced labor)
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of enslaved and impressed workers. Conditions were harsh and casualties were common.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When Wilson's cavalry burned Selma's foundries in April 1865, {ancestor_name}'s world — whether planter, enslaved, overseer, or yeoman — went up in the smoke. The Black Belt's antebellum economy did not decline. It was annihilated.
economic_eras.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994). Hardy, Selma and the Campaign for Alabama (2011).

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Sharecropping and tenant farming replacing slave labor; crop-lien credit system; Freedmen's Bureau labor contracts; violent suppression of Black political participation; persistent cotton dependence on declining soil
economic_eras.description: Emancipation freed over 100,000 enslaved people in the eight Black Belt counties but did not redistribute land or provide capital. The planter class retained land ownership; formerly enslaved people had labor but nothing else. The sharecropping system that emerged was a negotiated unfreedom: families worked a section of the plantation in exchange for a share of the crop (typically one-third to one-half), with the landowner or merchant advancing seed, tools, and supplies at interest rates exceeding 30%. The system was designed to keep labor on the land — and it worked. By 1880, sharecropping was near-universal in the Black Belt. Reconstruction brought the possibility of political change — Black voters constituted majorities in every county and elected Black officeholders to local and state positions. The response from the white planter class was systematic violence: the Ku Klux Klan, organized paramilitary groups, and targeted assassination of Black political leaders. By 1875, Reconstruction was effectively dead in the Alabama Black Belt, and by 1901, the new Alabama constitution had disenfranchised Black voters through poll taxes, literacy tests, and grandfather clauses.

county_specifics.note: Selma as Freedmen's Bureau district headquarters. Black political organizing strong but violently suppressed.
Sharecropping on the former Tombigbee plantations. Economic stagnation.
Violent Reconstruction politics. Black majority denied political expression.
Extreme Klan violence. Black officeholders driven from office through terrorism.
Lincoln Normal School (1867) provided Black education amid economic collapse.
County created 1867 during Reconstruction. Born into the sharecropping economy.
Gee's Bend families became sharecroppers on the Pettway land. Isolation deepened.
80%+ Black population denied all political power through violence and legal disenfranchisement.

key_infrastructure.name: Freedmen's Bureau offices
key_infrastructure.impact: Bureau offices at Selma, Demopolis, Eutaw, and other locations mediated labor contracts and provided limited protection for freedpeople.

key_infrastructure.name: Crop-lien merchant stores
key_infrastructure.impact: The crossroads store replaced the plantation commissary as the center of economic life. Merchants advanced supplies at usurious interest, creating debt peonage.

major_employers.name: Sharecropping on former plantations
major_employers.employment_scale: Near-universal among both Black and white farming families. The sharecropping system employed (trapped) the vast majority of the population.

wage_benchmarks.wage: Typically $0-50/year net; many ended the year in debt

economic_eras.narrative_hook: Sharecropping in the Black Belt was not farming — it was arithmetic designed to produce debt. {ancestor_name} worked the same dark soil under a new name for the old result: the crop went to the landowner, the interest went to the merchant, and the family got what was left, which was nothing.
economic_eras.source: Daniel, Breaking the Land (1985). Kolchin, First Freedom (1972). Foner, Reconstruction (1988).

economic_eras.era_name: Boll Weevil, Great Migration, and Economic Collapse
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Cotton in terminal decline; boll weevil destroying yields; Great Migration draining labor force; persistent sharecropping poverty; New Deal programs providing minimal relief; catfish farming beginning
economic_eras.description: The boll weevil entered Alabama from the west around 1910-1915, reaching the Black Belt counties by the late 1910s. Cotton yields collapsed by 30-50%. The sharecropping system, already producing poverty, became untenable. Black families began leaving in significant numbers — the Great Migration drew them to Birmingham's industrial jobs, and then to Chicago, Detroit, Cleveland, and other northern cities. The population of every Black Belt county declined in every census from 1900 to 1960. The New Deal brought some relief — the Farm Security Administration documented Hale County's poverty (Agee and Evans), and resettlement programs attempted to help Gee's Bend and other communities — but the fundamental economy was broken. The dark prairie soil that had produced fortunes in cotton was exhausted, the labor force was leaving, and no replacement economy emerged.

county_specifics.note: Selma's economy stagnated. Population declined. Some diversification into manufacturing.
Cotton collapse. Early catfish farming experiments in former cotton fields.
Among the poorest counties in Alabama by 1940.
Severe depopulation. County services deteriorated.
Marion's institutions provided some stability. Agricultural economy collapsed.
Agee and Evans documented the poverty in 1936. FSA attempted intervention.
Gee's Bend FSA resettlement project (1937). Ferry closure isolated community.
Population declining. Among the poorest counties in the United States.

key_infrastructure.name: Railroads (Southern Railway, Louisville & Nashville)
key_infrastructure.impact: The railroads that had shipped cotton now carried people north. The Great Migration used the same rail corridors that had served the cotton economy.

key_infrastructure.name: US Route 80
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected Selma to Montgomery. Would become the route of the 1965 voting rights marches.

major_employers.name: Sharecropping (declining)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining as families left. Fewer acres cultivated, fewer families working.

wage_benchmarks.wage: Typically $0-25/year net; debt was the norm

economic_eras.narrative_hook: The boll weevil finished what the Civil War started — {ancestor_name}'s cotton was half what the previous generation had produced, and the train that used to carry bales to Mobile now carried families to Birmingham and Chicago.
economic_eras.source: Daniel, Breaking the Land (1985). Grossman, Land of Hope (1989).

economic_eras.era_name: Civil Rights Era and the Long Struggle
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Continued agricultural decline; modest manufacturing at Selma; catfish farming emerging; war-related employment drawing workers away; civil rights organizing as a community-wide economic act (boycotts, voter registration requiring economic independence from white employers)
economic_eras.description: World War II and its aftermath accelerated the Great Migration — defense industry jobs in Mobile, Birmingham, and northern cities paid wages the Black Belt could not match. The postwar economy offered no recovery: cotton was not viable at competitive prices, the labor force was leaving, and no industrial employer chose to locate in the impoverished Black Belt. The civil rights movement, beginning in earnest in the early 1960s, had economic dimensions that are often overlooked: participating in voter registration drives or boycotts required economic independence from white employers — sharecroppers who registered to vote could be (and were) evicted from the land. The movement was therefore concentrated among independent farmers, teachers, ministers, and small business owners — the thin stratum of the Black community that had some economic autonomy.

county_specifics.note: Selma became the epicenter of the voting rights movement. Bloody Sunday (1965). Some diversification into manufacturing.
Catfish farming developing. Population declining.
University of West Alabama at Livingston. Persistent poverty.
Among the first AL counties to elect Black officeholders after the Voting Rights Act.
Jimmie Lee Jackson murdered (1965). Marion's institutions persisted.
Continued poverty. Minimal economic development.
Gee's Bend ferry restored (1966). Community organizing.
LCFO (Black Panther symbol). Murders of Daniels and Liuzzo (1965).

key_infrastructure.name: US Route 80 (Selma-Montgomery)
key_infrastructure.impact: The route of the Selma-to-Montgomery voting rights marches. Became the most famous highway in the civil rights movement.

key_infrastructure.name: Catfish ponds
key_infrastructure.impact: Former cotton fields converted to aquaculture. The beginning of a new agricultural economy that would replace cotton in the western Black Belt.

major_employers.name: Craig Air Force Base (Selma)
major_employers.employment_scale: Significant local employer providing non-agricultural jobs.

wage_benchmarks.source: Sugrue, Thomas. The Origins of the Urban Crisis. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1996.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: In {year}, the Black Belt's economy was not failing — it had already failed. {ancestor_name} lived in a county where the choice was clear: stay and work land that could no longer support a family, or follow the railroad north to wages the cotton had never matched.
economic_eras.source: Thornton, Dividing Lines (2002). Jeffries, Bloody Lowndes (2009).

economic_turning_points.event: Treaty of Fort Jackson — Creek cession opens the eastern Black Belt
economic_turning_points.impact: The land rush that created the plantation economy began with Creek dispossession. Within twenty years, the prairie was the richest cotton region in America.
economic_turning_points.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994).

economic_turning_points.event: Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek — Choctaw removal opens the western Black Belt
economic_turning_points.impact: Sumter and Greene counties opened to settlement. The western Black Belt's plantation economy developed in the 1830s.
economic_turning_points.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994).

economic_turning_points.event: Emancipation and Wilson's Raid — total economic collapse
economic_turning_points.impact: The loss of 100,000+ enslaved workers and the destruction of Selma's industrial base eliminated the antebellum economic system. The Black Belt never recovered its former wealth.

The 1901 constitution's poll tax, literacy test, and grandfather clause excluded the Black majority from political participation, ensuring that economic power remained in planter-class hands for another sixty years.
economic_turning_points.source: Rogers et al., Alabama: The History of a Deep South State (1994).

economic_turning_points.event: Boll weevil reaches the Black Belt
economic_turning_points.impact: Cotton yields collapsed 30-50%. The sharecropping system became untenable. The Great Migration accelerated.

economic_turning_points.event: Voting Rights Act — political transformation begins
economic_turning_points.impact: Black voter registration transformed the political landscape of the Black Belt. Economic transformation would follow more slowly, but the political precondition was met.

narrative_hooks: The Black Belt's wealth was never earned — it was extracted from enslaved people working dark prairie soil that belonged to someone else. {ancestor_name}'s labor produced cotton that made fortunes, none of which {pronoun_subject} shared.

Sharecropping in the Black Belt was not a step up from slavery — it was a renegotiation of the same arrangement. {ancestor_name} worked the same soil, lived in the same cabin, and owed the same landowner, the only difference being that the debt was now legal rather than existential.
The boll weevil did not kill the Black Belt economy — the economy was already dying of soil exhaustion and labor exploitation. The weevil was the last blow.

When {ancestor_name} left the Black Belt for Birmingham or Chicago in {year}, {pronoun_subject} was not abandoning home — {pronoun_subject} was leaving a place that had already abandoned its obligation to the people who had built it.

The Voting Rights Act of 1965 gave {ancestor_name}'s community the ballot — but a ballot without economic power was a start, not a solution. The Black Belt's poverty persisted because political rights alone could not replace the economic base that slavery had built and emancipation had destroyed.

In {county} County in {year}, the cotton economy was a century old and a century exhausted — {ancestor_name} worked soil that had been producing cotton since the Creek cession, and the dark clay was giving back less every decade.
source_references: Rogers, William Warren, Robert David Ward, Leah Rawls Atkins, and Wayne Flynt. Alabama: The History of a Deep South State. Tuscaloosa: University of Alabama Press, 1994.
Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land: The Transformation of Cotton, Tobacco, and Rice Cultures since 1880. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985.
Kolchin, Peter. First Freedom: The Responses of Alabama's Blacks to Emancipation and Reconstruction. Westport, CT: Greenwood Press, 1972.
Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South: Revolutions in the Southern Economy since the Civil War. New York: Basic Books, 1986.
Grossman, James R. Land of Hope: Chicago, Black Southerners, and the Great Migration. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1989.
Thornton, J. Mills, III. Dividing Lines: Municipal Politics and the Struggle for Civil Rights in Montgomery, Birmingham, and Selma. Tuscaloosa: University of Alabama Press, 2002.
Tadman, Michael. Speculators and Slaves: Masters, Traders, and Slaves in the Old South. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1989.
